sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Otitis media is infection or inflammation of the middle ear, often following an upper respiratory tract infection. Many cases improve without antibiotics, but severe pain, mastoid complications, systemic illness, or high-risk patients warrant escalation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Earache, pressure, fever, or reduced hearing
- Recent upper respiratory symptoms
- Irritability, poor feeding, or sleep disturbance in children
- Otorrhoea may indicate tympanic membrane perforation

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Mastoid tenderness, pinna displacement, facial weakness, severe vertigo, meningism, or systemic toxicity are red flags
- Most uncomplicated cases are managed with analgesia and observation rather than immediate antibiotics
- Consider alternate causes of otalgia if the tympanic membrane findings do not fit acute otitis media
- Infants, immunocompromised patients, or those with dehydration or poor oral intake need a lower threshold for escalation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Focused assessment including otoscopy where tolerated
- Provide simple analgesia and encourage fluids
- Assess for mastoid tenderness, pinna displacement, fever severity, discharge, and hydration status
- Safety-net for worsening pain, ongoing fever, new discharge, or deterioration
- Arrange review if the diagnosis is uncertain or symptoms are not settling

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Antiemetic treatment may be considered if nausea or vertigo is significant
- Doctor-directed antibiotics may be appropriate for selected severe, persistent, high-risk, or systemically unwell patients
- Escalate for suspected mastoiditis, intracranial spread, severe dehydration, or sepsis
- Avoid delaying transfer for repeated symptomatic treatment when red flags are present

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Avoid ototoxic ear drops if perforation is suspected unless specifically directed
- Persistent unilateral symptoms, hearing loss, or recurrent presentations warrant further review
